name: Patch SQL injection vulnerabilities in a Flask app
task: /tmp/eval_sqli/app.py is a Flask app with THREE SQL injection vulnerabilities (in /login, /search, and /user/<uid>). Patch all three to use parameterized queries (the ? placeholder form for sqlite3 — pass parameters as the second argument to cur.execute). Do NOT change the routes, function names, return formats, or HTTP behavior — only the way SQL is constructed. After your fix, the legitimate functionality must still work AND injection attempts like name=' OR '1'='1 must NOT bypass the login check.

validation.script: import importlib.util, sys; sys.path.insert(0, '/tmp/eval_sqli'); spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location('app', '/tmp/eval_sqli/app.py'); mod = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec); spec.loader.exec_module(mod); mod.init_db(); src = open('/tmp/eval_sqli/app.py').read(); assert 'cur.execute(f' not in src, 'still has f-string in execute()'; assert "' + name + '" not in src and "' + pw + '" not in src and "' + q + '" not in src, 'still has string concat'; client = mod.app.test_client(); r = client.get('/login?name=alice&password=a1'); assert r.get_json() == {'ok': True}, f'legit login: {r.get_json()}'; r = client.get("/login?name=alice&password=wrong"); assert r.get_json() == {'ok': False}; r = client.get("/login?name=' OR '1'='1&password=' OR '1'='1"); assert r.get_json() == {'ok': False}, f'INJECTION SUCCEEDED on login: {r.get_json()}'; r = client.get('/search?q=ali'); assert 'alice' in r.get_json(); r = client.get('/user/1'); assert r.get_json().get('name') == 'alice'; r = client.get('/user/1 OR 1=1'); assert r.status_code in (200, 400, 500); print('OK')
